Cache

System Design Interview

Programming

Coding

Interview

7 min read
Just now

--

Press enter or click to view image in full size

Photo by He Junhui on Unsplash
I’m starting a structured High-Level Design learning journey to strengthen my system design skills and prepare for backend engineering interviews. In this series, I’ll share concise notes, key concepts, and design trade-offs as I learn them. Day 7 focuses on the Cache Patterns & Cache Consistency.
In high-level design, choosing the correct cache pattern and maintaining data consistency determines whether a system remains resilient under load or collapses into state corruption.
Cache Patterns
1. Cache-Aside (Lazy Loading)
The application orchestrates all data movement between the cache and the database. The cache does not talk directly to the database.
Architecture & Request Flow:

• Application checks the cache.

• Cache Hit: Data is returned directly to the client.

• Cache Miss: Application reads from the database, writes the data to the cache, and returns it to the client.
[Client] -----> [Application] ----(1. Read Hit)----> [Cache]
| ^
|---(2. Read Miss)---> [DB] |
| |
|<------(3. Return Data)----------|
| |
|----(4. Write Missing Data)------|
• Advantages: Resilient to cache failures (system falls back to DB); memory-efficient (only requested data is cached).

• Disadvantages: Cache miss penalty (three steps for missed data); data staleness if the database is updated directly without invalidating the cache.

• Best Use Cases: Read-heavy workloads with unpredictable access patterns (e.g., user profiles).

• Companies Using It: Facebook, Memcached-centric architectures.

• Interview Mistakes: Forgetting that concurrent writes can cause race conditions between updating the database and evicting the old cache key.
2. Read-Through
The application treats the cache as the main data store. On a miss, the cache infrastructure itself fetches data from the database.
Architecture & Request Flow:

• Application requests data from the cache library/provider.

• Cache Hit: Cache returns data.

• Cache Miss: Cache provider transparently queries the database, populates itself, and returns data to the application.
[Client] ----> [Application] ----(1. Read)----> [Cache]
|
(2. Cache Miss)
v
[DB]
• Advantages: Simplifies application code (no cache management logic); optimized for read-heavy flows.

• Disadvantages: High latency on the first request for a key; requires a dedicated cache provider plugin/library supporting data syncing.

• Best Use Cases: Systems where the same data is read repeatedly immediately after creation.

• Companies Using It: Large-scale enterprise systems using tools like NCache or Oracle Coherence.

• Interview Mistakes: Treating Read-Through and Cache-Aside as identical; in Read-Through, the cache queries the DB, not the application layer.
3. Write-Through
Data is written to the cache and the database synchronously within the same write path.

• Architecture & Request Flow:

• Application issues a write request.

• Cache intercepts the write, updates its own state, and synchronously writes to the database.

• The write operation returns success only after both stores are updated.
[Client] ----> [Application] ----(1. Write)----> [Cache] ----(2. Sync Write)----> [DB]
| |
[Application] <---(3. Success)-------|<------(Ack Success)----------|
• Advantages: Zero stale reads; cache data is always up to date.

• Disadvantages: High write latency due to two synchronous write operations.

• Best Use Cases: Systems requiring strict data consistency paired with frequent subsequent reads.

• Companies Using It: Financial ledgers, banking applications.

• Interview Mistakes: Recommending this for write-heavy systems without acknowledging the severe latency penalty on mutations.
4. Write-Around
Data is written directly to the database, bypassing the cache entirely during mutations.

• Architecture & Request Flow:

• Application writes data directly to the database.

• The cache key is either left empty or explicitly invalidated (deleted).

• The data enters the cache only on a subsequent read miss.
[Client] ----> [Application] ----(1. Direct Write)----------------------> [DB]
| |
|----(2. Optional Invalidate / Leave Empty)----> [Cache] |
| |
|<------------(3. Cache Miss reads from DB)-----------|
• Advantages: Prevents “cache pollution” (filling the cache with data that may not be read anytime soon).

• Disadvantages: A read directly following a write guarantees a cache miss.

• Best Use Cases: Real-time video uploads, data logging, or batch uploads where data isn’t read immediately after creation.

• Companies Using It: YouTube (video metadata/uploads), Netflix (movie catalog ingestion).

• Interview Mistakes: Failing to couple Write-Around with an explicit cache invalidation step, leading to stale data reads on existing keys.
5. Write-Back (Write-Behind)
Data is written synchronously to the cache, and asynchronously flushed to the database after a delay.

• Architecture & Request Flow:

• Application writes to the cache. Cache acknowledges instantly.

• An asynchronous worker thread or queue pulls dirty keys from the cache and batches writes to the database.
[Client] ----> [Application] ----(1. Write)----> [Cache] (Instantly Ack Success)
|
(2. Async Batch)
v
[Queue/Worker] ----> [DB]
• Advantages: Exceptionally low write latency; absorbs massive write spikes by coalescing multiple updates into a single DB write.

• Disadvantages: High risk of data loss if the cache node crashes before the asynchronous flush completes.

• Best Use Cases: High-throughput write workloads where temporary data loss is acceptable (e.g., gaming leaderboards, real-time analytics counters).

• Companies Using It: Zynga, IoT telemetry ingestion pipelines.

• Interview Mistakes: Proposing this pattern for critical financial data without addressing single-point-of-failure (SPOF) risks or node crashes.
6. Refresh-Ahead
The cache proactively reloads frequently accessed keys before they expire based on access analytics.
Architecture & Request Flow:

• The cache tracks hot keys and their configured Time-To-Live (TTL).

• If a hot key is predicted to expire soon, an asynchronous background job queries the database to refresh the cache key.
(Background Job Tracks TTL)
[Client] ----> [Background Job] ----(1. Predictive Check)----> [Cache Hot Keys]
| ^
|-------------(2. Read from DB)-------> [DB] --|
• Advantages: Eliminates read latency and avoids cache stampedes for popular keys.

• Disadvantages: Can waste database resources if the predictive algorithm incorrectly guesses which keys will remain active.

• Best Use Cases: Highly predictable read patterns (e.g., daily news homepages, stock tickers).

• Companies Using It: Large content portals, financial market dashboards.

• Interview Mistakes: Forgetting that this requires an external tracking or prediction mechanism, adding structural architectural complexity.

Cache Consistency
Maintaining a unified state between your persistent storage and ephemeral cache layer is dominated by trade-offs outlined in the CAP Theorem.
Consistency Models
1. Strong Consistency:
Guarantees that any read operation returns the result of the latest committed write.
Trade-off: High latency, requires distributed locks or synchronous two-phase commits.
2. Eventual Consistency:
Reads may temporarily return stale data, but all replicas and caches will eventually converge to the latest state.
Trade-off: Low latency, requires application tolerance for momentary out-of-sync reads.
3. Read-After-Write (Read-Your-Own-Writes):
A specific constraint guaranteeing that if User A updates a record, User A will always see that update on subsequent reads. Other users might experience eventual consistency.

Anomalies

• Stale Reads: A client retrieves outdated data from the cache because the underlying database was updated without updating or invalidating the cache.

• Race Conditions: Concurrent requests execute interleaving reads and writes, leading to old data overwriting newer data inside the cache.

Cache Invalidation
“There are only two hard things in Computer Science: cache invalidation and naming things.” > — Phil Karlton
1. TTL (Time-To-Live)
An automated expiration window attached to a cache key.

• Mechanism: Passive invalidation. When the timer expires, the key is evicted, forcing a database fetch on the next read.

• When to use: Good for non-critical data or as a safety net alongside active invalidation strategies.
2. Manual Invalidation
The application code explicitly deletes a cache key when a corresponding database mutation occurs.

• Mechanism: Active invalidation. Usually triggered directly inside the application service layer using cache.delete(key).

• When to use: Used in Cache-Aside or Write-Around patterns where immediate consistency is preferred.
3. Event-Driven Invalidation
Decouples database updates from cache clearing via an asynchronous message bus.

• Mechanism: Change Data Capture (CDC) tools (e.g., Debezium) stream commit logs from the database to a message queue (e.g., Kafka). A worker consumes the events and invalidates relevant cache keys.

• When to use: Complex microservice architectures where multiple services depend on the same underlying database state.
4. Versioning / Cache Busting
Modifying the identity of the resource instead of modifying the cache state directly.

• Mechanism: Appending a unique version identifier, hash, or timestamp to the cache key (e.g., user:101:v2). Old keys are left to expire naturally via TTL or LRU evictions.

• When to use: Asset delivery (CDNs), static files, or immutable data entities.

Mind Map Blueprint
Use this structural outline to anchor your revision:
Cache Management
├── Patterns
│ ├── Read-Heavy: Cache-Aside, Read-Through, Refresh-Ahead
│ └── Write-Heavy: Write-Through, Write-Around, Write-Back
├── Consistency
│ ├── Models: Strong, Eventual, Read-Your-Own-Writes
│ └── Anomalies: Stale Reads, Race Conditions
└── Invalidation
├── Passive: TTL
├── Active: Manual, Event-Driven (CDC)
└── Structural: Versioning / Cache Busting
Interview quetions

• Q1: Your database CPU suddenly spikes to 95% after a cache node failure in a Cache-Aside setup. How do you mitigate this dynamically without restarting the database?

• Q2: In a Cache-Aside pattern, User A updates their profile description while millions are reading it. Describe the precise sequence of operations to prevent race conditions.

• Q3: How do you handle a scenario where the database update succeeds, but the subsequent cache eviction (cache.delete(key)) fails?

• Q4: A massive e-commerce platform needs to track flash sale inventory updates in real-time. Which cache pattern do you pick, and why?

• Q5: You are designing a high-throughput IoT telemetry ingestion system processing 500,000 writes per second. The storage layer cannot handle this IOPS. How do you optimize this using caching?

• Q6: For a video platform like YouTube, users upload thousands of videos that might not be viewed for days. Which pattern prevents cache pollution here?

• Q7: A user updates their profile picture on Instagram but immediately refreshes the page and still sees their old photo. What anomaly is this, and how do you fix it structurally?

• Q8: Explain how you would implement Strong Consistency in a distributed cache environment when executing writes.

• Q9: Your system design requires near-zero staleness across dozens of downstream microservices consuming the same database state. Why is Manual Invalidation a bad choice, and what should you use instead?

• Q10: What is “Cache Busting,” and how do you leverage it when deploying updated static assets globally across a CDN?
